segments.s037-g65: This is a noun phrase with two adjectives preceding the counted noun: 'گرانمایه و پاک' (precious and pure) modify 'هرسه پسر' (all three sons). 'هرسه' is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'all three' (هر + سه) and appears before the head noun, a common older Persian order.
segments.s037-g66: 'همه' quantifies the group ('all of them'), and 'دل‌نهاده' is a adjectival/past-participial form from دل نهادن ('to set one's heart on'), meaning 'having devoted their hearts'. The prepositional phrase 'به گفت پدر' ('to/for the father's speech/words') is the complement showing what they are devoted to; Persian often omits a copula, so this whole segment serves as the predicate describing the sons.
segments.s037-g67: The prepositional phrase 'ز پیش فریدون' means 'from the presence of Fereydun' (ز + پیش = 'from before / out of the presence of'), followed by the past plural verb 'برون آمدند' ('they came/stepped out'). Word order places the location/source before the verb, and the verb is marked plural to agree with the (implicit) plural subject.
segments.s037-g68: 'پر از' + noun is an adjectival construction meaning 'full of ...' (here 'پر از دانش' = 'full of knowledge'). The coordinating 'و پرفسون' adds the second quality; the clause closes with the repeated past plural verb 'آمدند' ('they came'), a stylistic repetition that emphasizes their arrival in that condition.
segments.s037-g69: 'بجز رای و دانش' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'apart from / except for counsel and knowledge.' The interrogative 'چه اندرخورد' ('what is fitting/befitting') asks for anything else appropriate; 'اندرخورد' is an older poetic adjective/participle meaning 'befitting' and serves as the predicate of the question.
segments.s037-g70: Here 'پسر را' marks 'the son' as a definite/affected item (را = object/definiteness marker). The relative particle 'که' introduces the clause that modifies 'پسر'; 'چونان پدر پرورد' means 'whom (the) father nurtured/raised like himself.' The verb 'پرورد' is past tense (3rd person) of 'پروردن' ('to nurture/raise') with its subject understood from context (the father).
segments.s037-g33: Literal order places the predicative adjective phrase before the noun: "پر از آفرین لب" = "lip(s) full of praise" (پر here = 'full (of)'). "ز ایوان اوی" means "from his iwan/portico"; ایوان is 'iwan/porch' and اوی is an archaic possessive (= 'his'). The whole segment describes his state/location as he prepares to move.
segments.s037-g34: سوی + NP marks direction ('toward'). شهریار جهان is a title 'the king/lord of the world'. کرد روی is a light-verb construction: کرد (past) + روی ('face') = 'turned (his) face'. The verb is simple past; word order is prepositional phrase + object/title + verb phrase.
segments.s037-g35: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' here. نزد + name means 'in the presence of' or 'to' (i.e. he arrived before Fereydun). رسید is another past verb 'reached/arrived' — the two verbs together give the temporal sequence ('he came; when he reached Fereydun').
segments.s037-g36: بگفت is past 'he said' and here introduces direct speech. آن is the demonstrative 'that' (part of the quoted clause), کجا is the interrogative 'where', and گفت is used as a noun ('what was said' / 'the saying') — so the phrase amounts to asking 'Where was that said?' تا/و then و connects to the final clause, and پاسخ شنید = 'he heard the answer' (شنید simple past of 'to hear').
segments.s037-g37: سه فرزند را is the definite/accusative object ('three children/sons' with the object marker را placed after it). خواند here means 'summoned/called' (past). شاه جهان follows the verb as the subject in poetic/inverted order: 'the king of the world called (them)'.
segments.s037-g38: This is an imperative instruction: نهفته 'hidden' describes the ones to be brought; برون آورید is 2nd-person plural imperative 'bring out/bring forth'. از نهان = 'from concealment/from hiding' (prepositional phrase giving origin). The order is adjective + imperative + origin.
segments.s037-g39: از آن here means 'concerning that' or 'about that'. رفتن is an infinitive used nominally = 'the going/departure'. جندل is the name (here referring to the minstrel Jandal); because the provided sense list does not include a proper-name sense, that entry is marked -1. و links to رای خویش 'and his own counsel/plan' (خوی + ش is split: خوی root, ش enclitic = 'his'). The phrase lists the topics he addressed.
segments.s037-g40: سخنها همه = 'all the words/speeches' (fronted object with همِه emphasizing totality). پاک here modifies the verb (meaning 'clearly/plainly' or 'without concealment'). بنهاد is past-tense 'he placed/laid (forth)' (from بنهادن), and پیش means 'before / in the presence of' (i.e. he laid them out openly before those present).
segments.s037-g17: به فرمان شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “by the command of the king” (به used in the instrumental/agency sense). این three‑noun sequence follows Persian order: demonstrative این + numeral/quantifier سه + noun فرزند, with the possessor من placed after the noun (فرزند من = “my child/children”).
segments.s037-g18: برون آنگه آید ز پیوند من: برون (out/forth) is an adverbial; آنگه (then) is a temporal/discourse adverb; آید is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive from آمدن used here as a future/neutral ‘will/shall come’. ز is the poetic preposition “from,” and پیوند من = “my bond/lineage” (پیوند here = lineage/connection). Word order is adverb(s) + verb + source phrase.
segments.s037-g19: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’. من is the explicit subject ‘I’. ببینم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive used here idiomatically as a future/interrogative (‘shall I see’ / ‘may I see’). سه شاه ترا is the object: سه شاه = ‘three kings’, with the 2nd‑person enclitic ت written separately (ت = your) and را the definite/object marker (here written as ترا in poetry) marking the object.
segments.s037-g20: فروزنده is a present participle/adjective ‘illuminating, brightening’ that modifies the following noun phrase. تاج و گاه ترا = ‘your crown and your seat/time’ (گاه here functioning as ‘seat/throne’ in context); the second‑person enclitic ت again marks possession and را is the object marker. The whole phrase characterizes the kings as those who make the addressee’s crown and throne shine.
segments.s037-g21: بیایند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/jussive (‘may they come’ / ‘if they come’) often used in conditional protases in poetry. هر سه = ‘all three’ / ‘each of the three’. به نزدیک من = ‘to/near me’ (به + نزدیک as a directional phrase, with من as the object meaning ‘near me’).
segments.s037-g22: شود is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن ‘become’ placed before the predicate (inverted order typical in poetry). روشن is the predicate adjective ‘bright’. این شهر تاریک من = ‘this dark city of mine’ with the possessive من following the noun; together they are the subject that becomes روشن.
segments.s037-g23: شود شادمان دل is another verb‑first inversion: شود (becomes) + شادمان (joyful) with دل (the heart) as the logical subject/experiencer — ‘the heart becomes joyful’. به دیدارشان is a prepositional phrase ‘at/for their meeting/sight of them’, with شان marking ‘their’ and linking this clause to the following sight.
segments.s037-g24: ببینم is 1st‑person singular subjunctive/optative (‘may I see’ / ‘let me see’). روانهای بیدارشان is the object: روانهای (plural ‘souls/spirits’) modified by بیدارشان (‘their awake/awakened’), with شان again the 3rd‑person plural possessive enclitic attached to the adjective.
segments.s037-g25: ببینم is a 1st‑person present verb (poetic form without the modern mi‑ prefix) meaning 'I see'. کشان is a participial/adverbial form ('‑keshān') meaning 'pulling/drawing' and here links the seeing to a manner or concurrent action. دل پر از داد هست is a small clause: دل 'heart' + پر از 'full of' + داد 'justice', closed by the copula هست 'is' — literally 'the heart is full of justice'. The participle modifies the scene described by the following nominal clause.
segments.s037-g26: This unit centers on the verb گیرم (I take/seize). زنهارشان is a noun 'their zanhār' (warning/precaution) placed before the object to indicate purpose or manner ('as a warning' / 'for their safety'). دست گیرم literally 'I take (the) hand' (دست is the direct object of گیرم). The following به دست functions as an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with (my) hand' or a reinforcing 'hand in hand'. Word order places the purposive phrase (به زنهارشان) before the verb phrase.
segments.s037-g27: پس آنگه is a redundant pair of temporal adverbs ('then' / 'thereupon') used for emphasis. سه روشن جهان‌بین خویش is a noun phrase: سه روشن 'three bright/enlightened' modifies جهان‌بین 'world‑seer' (an epithet). خویش is the possessive element ('one's own') split here into خوی + ش (his/her/own), so the whole phrase means 'my three bright world‑seers'.
segments.s037-g28: سپارم is a 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust/leave'. بدیشان ('be‑dīshān') is the prepositional object 'to them'. بر آیین خویش is a prepositional phrase 'according to/on (my) custom' where بر + آیین marks the standard or mode and خویش (khvish = 'one's own') is the possessive; word order is typical Persian: verb then indirect object then oblique phrase.
segments.s037-g29: چو آید is a temporal subordinate ('when it comes'). بدیدار ایشان is an object phrase 'the meeting/seeing of them' (بدیدار = 'the visit/meeting', ایشان = 'them'). نیاز here means 'longing/desire' — the whole phrase means 'when the longing to see them comes', with Persian placing the temporal subordinate before the main clause.
segments.s037-g30: فرستم is 'I send' (1st‑person). سبکشان ('sabk‑shān') here functions adverbially/objetively as 'them lightly' or 'their lightness'—in practice 'I send them lightly' (شان marks 'them'). سوی شاه gives direction 'toward the king' and باز 'back/again' marks the return; the verb governs the (implicit) direct object 'them' realized in the clitic on سبکشان.
segments.s037-g31: This is a temporal clause. سراینده is a noun 'the singer/poet'. جندل here is a name or epithet attached to the poet (given senses include 'rock' or 'small troop', but in context it functions as the poet's epithet/place name). چو آید/شنید pattern: چو + past verb (شنید 'he heard') marks 'when he heard'. پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply' and is the object of شنید.
segments.s037-g32: ببوسید is a past/preterite form 'he kissed' (classical narrative past). تختش is 'his throne/seat' (تخت + possessive ش). چنان چون introduces a comparative formula 'in such a way as' (چنان ... چون), and سزید here means 'to deserve/that which is deserved' — together 'he kissed his throne as it deserved' i.e. 'as was fitting'.
segments.s037-g9: سخن هر چه گفتی پذیرم همی = a single clause: سخن ('words/speech') is the topical noun, followed by a free relative phrase هر چه گفتی ('whatever you said/whatever you say'). پذیرم is first-person singular present/future 'I accept'; the enclitic همی is an archaic verbal particle that makes the verb iterative/continuous or emphatic (classical Persian usage: ‹I will/indeed accept›). Word order is topic — relative — verb, common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s037-g10: ز دختر من اندازه گیرم همی = 'ز' (poetic preposition) introduces the standard/measure ('by/from my daughter'). اندازه گیرم is a verbal phrase 'I take/measure/judge' (اندازه گرفتن = to measure, to judge); the verb is first-person singular. همی again is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle attached to the verb. The object/possessor order (دختر من = my daughter) is postnominal, as in Persian.
segments.s037-g11: اگر پادشا دیده خواهد ز من = conditional clause. اگر marks 'if'; پادشا is the subject 'the king'. دیده خواهد is an archaic/poetic ordering where the past participle (دیده) precedes the auxiliary خواهد (compare modern خواهد دید), so it means 'wishes/will see'. ز من ('from me' / 'me') is the object introduced by poetic z. The overall sense is 'if the king wishes to see me'.
segments.s037-g12: و‌گر دشت گردان و تخت یمن = continuation/alternative in the conditional: و‌گر = 'or if'. دشت گردان is a compound-like phrase meaning 'those (who) roam the plains' or 'the roving/plain-roving (forces)' where گردان functions adjectivally/participially; تخت یمن 'the throne of Yemen' is a coordinated noun phrase. The verb (خواهد/دیده) is omitted here and understood from the previous clause (ellipsis common in Persian poetry).
segments.s037-g13: مرا خوارتر چون سه فرزند خویش = مرا (object pronoun 'me', here topical/accusative) + خوارتر (comparative adjective 'more contemptible') + چون ('than' in comparisons) + سه فرزند خویش ('his three children', with خویش/ش marking 'his own'). The phrase states a comparison: 'me more contemptible than his three children.' Note that the segmentation in the word list splits مـ and را, but together function as the object pronoun 'مرا'.
segments.s037-g14: نبینم به هنگام بایست پیش = نبینم is first-person negative ('I do not/will not see' or 'I shall not consider'); به هنگام = 'at the time/when'; بایست here is the verb 'to stand' (classical form) rather than the modal 'must'—it governs پیش 'before/in the presence of'. Together: 'when they stand before (him)' or 'at the time they stand in his presence.' The verb for the comparison ('I will not see myself as more contemptible...') is negative and governs the temporal clause that follows.
segments.s037-g15: پس ار شاه را این‌چنین است کام = 'پس' ('then/therefore') + conditional ار ('if') introduces the condition. شاه را functions as the possessor/experiencer (classical word order: 'to the king there is such a desire') and این‌چنین است کام literally 'such is the desire'. In modern English: 'So, if the king's desire is like this...' Note the classical order where the possessor is marked with را and the predicate کام (desire) is linked by است.
segments.s037-g16: نشاید زدن جز به فرمانش گام = نشاید is an impersonal negative/judgmental verb 'it is not fitting/it should not be'. زدن + گام forms the idiom 'to take a step' (زدنِ گام = to make/move a step). جز به فرمانش = 'except by his command' (فرمانش = 'his command' with possessive suffix). The clause means 'one ought not to take a step except by his command.'
segments.s037-g41: چنین گفت کاین — Literally 'thus said that this'. چنين + گفت is a conventional narrative formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). گفت is the past verb 'he said' introducing reported content; کاین (ک + این) is the subordinating particle 'that/this' linking the speech to what follows. 'شهریار یمن' is the head noun phrase 'king of Yemen' in noun + modifier order (شهریار 'king' followed by یمن 'of Yemen').
segments.s037-g42: سر انجمن سرو سایه فکن — A string of nominal/epithet phrases in apposition. سر انجمن = 'chief/head of the assembly' (سر here = 'chief'). سرو سایه‌فکن is a compound epithet: سرو = 'cypress' and سایه فکن 'shade-casting' (فکن used adjectivally = 'casting/throwing' [shade]). The whole cluster apposes/characterises the شهریار introduced before.
segments.s037-g43: چو ناسفته گوهر سه دخترش بود — چو introduces a temporal/conditional 'when/as'. ناسفته is a poetic participial adjective 'unwrought/untouched' modifying گوهر 'gem'. The following noun phrase سه دخترش بود uses بود (past of بودن) as the existential/predicative verb 'there was / he had' to state 'he had three daughters'. Word order: descriptive adjectives precede the noun they modify in such poetic sequences.
segments.s037-g44: نبودش پسر دختر افسرش بود — This contains two short clauses. نبودش پسر = 'he had no son' (نبودش = 'was not/had not' with enclitic pronoun ش). The next clause دختر افسرش بود = 'his daughter was Afsar' (افسرش here functions as a name/epithet attached with possessive -ش; بود again is the past copula 'was'). The two clauses are juxtaposed to contrast lack of a son with the presence/name of a daughter.
segments.s037-g45: سروش ار بیابد چو ایشان عروس — ار is the conditional particle 'if'; بیابد is the classical/poetic subjunctive/present form of 'to find' (here 'should find' / 'finds'). چو here functions as 'as/that' introducing the result/role ('as brides') and ایشان is the object 'them' or 'those (girls)'. The clause says 'If Soroush finds them (to be) brides...'.
segments.s037-g46: دهد پیش هر یک مگر خاک‌بوس — دهد (present/subjunctive form of دادن) is used here in narrative to mean 'he will give' or 'gives'; پیش = 'before/in the presence of' and هر یک = 'each one' (each suitor). مگر = 'except' and خاک‌بوس is an epithet 'dust-kisser' (one who prostrates/kisses the earth). Together: 'he will give (them) before each (one), except the dust-kisser.'
segments.s037-g47: ز بهر شما از پدر خواستم — ز بهر شما = 'for your sake' (ز...بهر is a poetic way to say 'for/on behalf of you'). از پدر = 'from the father' (the source or addressee of the request). خواستم is the 1st-person past 'I asked/wished' (here 'I asked [the father]'). Word order is topical: 'for your sake' fronted, then the source 'from the father', then the verb.
segments.s037-g48: سخنهای بایسته آراستم — سخنهای (the plural/collective form 'words/speeches') modified by بایسته 'proper/necessary'. آراستم is a first-person perfect/simple-past 'I have arranged/put together' (from آرودن/آراستن) meaning 'I composed/put in order'. The clause states 'I have arranged proper words' — a claim of having prepared suitable remarks.
segments.s037-g49: کنون (now) sets the temporal frame; تان is the dative/benefactive 'to you' (archaic/formal). بباید is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary / you must', followed by the infinitival/nominal clause بر او شدن 'to become/turn toward him' (بر = toward + او = him + شدن = become/attach), so the whole phrase means 'now you must turn to him.'
segments.s037-g50: به هر بیش و کم is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in/for every greater and lesser [matter]' (به + هر + بیش و کم = in all matters, big and small). رای فرخ زدن is a verbal phrase meaning 'take/offer/adhere to Farrokh's counsel' (رای = counsel/plan, فرخ used as an epithet/name 'Farrokh' or 'the fortunate one', and زدن here is a light verb meaning 'put/offer/declare' in the sense 'adopt/express the counsel').
segments.s037-g51: سراینده باشید is an imperative: سراینده 'poet/minstrel' + باشید (2nd‑person plural/formal imperative of بودن) 'be'. و بسیارهوش links a coordinating predicate 'and be very wise/clever' (بسیارهوش functions as a compound adjective/adverbial description).
segments.s037-g52: به گفتار او is a prepositional object 'to/for his speech' (i.e., with respect to what he says). برنهاده دوگوش is a verbal/participial phrase literally 'having placed two ears' — idiomatically 'set both ears (to it)', where برنهاده = placed/put and دوگوش = 'two‑eared' used metaphorically 'attentive'. The clause thus commands attentive listening.
segments.s037-g53: به خوبی is an adverbial expression 'well' ('به' + خوبی gives the manner). سخنهاش (سخن + ها + ـش) is 'his words/speeches' and پاسخ دهید is the polite/plural imperative 'give an answer / reply'. The order is typical Persian: manner + object + verb 'reply well to his words.'
segments.s037-g54: چو پرسد is a temporal subordinate 'when he asks' (چو = 'when'; پرسد = 'he asks'). سخن is the object 'what he asks about'; رای فرخ نهید is an imperative/optative clause 'set/offer Farrokh's counsel' (رای = counsel/plan; فرخ = the fortunate/Farrokh; نهید is the 2nd‑person plural/optative form 'put/offer').
segments.s037-g55: ازیرا که is an archaic/causal connector here meaning 'for/since' (introducing the reason). پرورده پادشا is a participial phrase 'reared/nurtured by the king' (پرورده = reared, پادشا = king), so the clause gives the ground: 'since he was reared by the king...'.
segments.s037-g56: نباید که باشد is an impersonal negative modal: نباید 'ought not / should not' + که باشد (subjunctive 'that he be'), i.e. 'it ought not to be that...'. به جز پارسا is the exception phrase 'except a pious one' (به جز = except, پارسا = pious). Together: 'there ought to be none except a pious person.'
segments.s037-g57: A nominal phrase listing three epithets joined by و (and). Each item is a compound adjective/noun: سخن‌گو (an eloquent/speaking one), روشن‌دل (literally 'clear‑hearted'), پاک‌دین (pious/devout). Word order is straightforward adjective/noun list; this phrase functions as a description of the person who is then characterized by the following clause.
segments.s037-g58: A prepositional phrase + relative clause: به کاری 'in/with regard to a matter', که introduces a relative clause 'that/which', پیش آیدش = پیش آید + enclitic -ش ('comes/befalls him'), and پیش‑بین is a predicate noun/adjective 'one who foresees' (placed after the clause for poetic word order). The whole means 'foreseeing with respect to any affair that befalls him.' Note the enclitic -ش marks 'to him' and the relative clause modifies کاری.
segments.s037-g59: زبان راستی را is a noun phrase 'the speech/tongue of truth' with را (object/definite marker) after راستی to emphasize the phrase; بیاراسته here is a predicative participle/adjective meaning 'arrayed/adorned' (poetic word order places the predicate after the object phrase). Note that in poetic usage the usual ezāfe linking can be compressed and the object marker را is used for emphasis.
segments.s037-g60: This is a compact, poetic clause: خرد 'wisdom' as subject, خیره کرده a compound participle meaning 'having made (people) stare' or 'astonishing', and ابر خواسته a figurative compound ('ابر' + 'خواسته') — literally 'cloud‑desired' — used here as a lofty/loftily desired quality. The sequence places nouns and participles in apposition; the whole describes the wonder and loftiness of his intellect.
segments.s037-g61: A two‑part imperative/optative construction: شما 'you (pl.)' is the addressee; هر چه گویم is a subordinate clause 'whatever I say' with گویم in the present/subjunctive (used here to express what the speaker utters); ز من بشنوید uses the poetic preposition ز 'from' + من 'me' and بشنوید (2nd pl. imperative/subjunctive) meaning 'hear [it] from me.' The clause requests the audience to heed the speaker's words.
segments.s037-g62: A conditional clause: اگر 'if', کار بندید where کار 'the affair/task' is the object and بندید (2nd person plural subjunctive/imperative form of بستن in older usage) means 'you accomplish/complete' or more literally 'you bind/settle' the work; خرم بوید is a concluding prediction with خرم 'joyful/prosperous' and بوید an archaic verbal form meaning 'it will be' (classical/poetic usage). Together: 'If you do the work, it will be prosperous.'
segments.s037-g63: An existential/introduction phrase with inverted order: یکی 'one/a certain (person)', ژرف‑بین an epithet 'deep‑seeing/insightful', است 'is', and شاه یمن 'the king of Yemen' as predicate nominative. The inversion (epithet before است) is typical of poetic style to foreground the characteristic before giving the identity/title.
segments.s037-g64: A subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/for': چون here functions temporally/causally ('when' / 'as'), او 'he' the third‑person pronoun, نباشد an archaic negative subjunctive/present ('is not present' / 'there is not'), به a preposition introducing the location/setting, and هرانجمن 'every assembly/gathering'. The clause means 'for when he is not present at any assembly...' (the thought continues).
segments.s037-g1: فرستاده شاه را پیش خواند = 'he summoned the king's envoy forward.' پیش + خواند is a verbal phrase meaning 'call forward/summon.' خواند is a past-tense 3rd-person singular; شاه را here identifies the envoy as belonging to the king (poetic word order places the possessor phrase with را), and فرستاده is the definite noun 'the envoy/messenger.'
segments.s037-g2: فراوان سخن را به خوبی براند = 'he uttered many words very well.' فراوان functions adverbially ('plentifully/many'), سخن را is the definite object marked by را, به خوبی is an adverbial phrase ('well' — به + خوبی), and براند is a verb here meaning 'render/utter/put forth' (3rd-person, classical usage) modifying what was said.
segments.s037-g3: که من شهریار ترا کهترم = 'that I, your sovereign, am humbler.' که introduces the reported content; من is the 1st-person subject; شهریار + ت (the 2nd-person enclitic) yields 'your sovereign/king' addressed to the addressee; را follows for definiteness/emphasis; کهترم is the predicate 'I am lesser/inferior' with the 1st-person ending -م.
segments.s037-g4: به هرچ او بفرمود فرمانبرم = 'in whatever he ordered, I am obedient.' به + هرچ = 'to/for whatever' (introducing a free relative), او is the 3rd-person subject of بفرمود (an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'), and فرمانبرم is a predicative adjective 'I am obedient' with the 1st-person ending -م.
segments.s037-g5: بگویش که گرچه تو هستی بلند = 'Tell him that although you are lofty.' بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic -ش = 'to him'); که introduces the reported clause; گرچه is a concessive 'although/even though'; تو هستی is the 2nd-person present 'you are'; بلند is the predicate adjective 'high/lofty.'
segments.s037-g6: سه فرزند تو برتو بر ارجمند = 'your three sons are more esteemed than you.' سه فرزند تو is the subject ('your three children/sons'); برتو (on/over you) plus the comparative sense of the following بر gives the comparison 'over/against you'; ارجمند is the predicate 'esteemed/precious.' The doubled prepositional sequence (برتو بر) is a poetic comparative construction.
segments.s037-g7: پسر خود گرامی بود شاه را = 'the king's own son was dear to him.' پسر خود = 'his own son' (خود intensifies possession), گرامی بود is the copular predicate 'was dear/valued,' and شاه را (word order inverted) marks the experiencer 'to the king' with را used for emphasis/definiteness.
segments.s037-g8: بویژه که زیبا بود گاه را = 'especially because he was handsome.' بویژه is an adverb 'especially'; که introduces the reason ('because'); زیبا بود is 'was beautiful/handsome' (past copula); گاه را is a poetic/idiomatic addition (گاه literally 'time/occasion' used here with را for emphasis, functioning adverbially/idiomatically to qualify the preceding clause).

sentences.s037-s33: All three sons, precious and pure, were wholly devoted to their father's words.
sentences.s037-s34: They stepped out from before Fereydun; they came filled with knowledge and 'pərfuson'.
sentences.s037-s35: Apart from counsel and learning, what else is fitting for the son whom the father raised like himself?
sentences.s037-s17: With his lips full of praise, he turned from his iwan and faced the king of the world.
sentences.s037-s18: He came; when he reached Fereydun he said, "Where was that said?" and he heard the answer.
sentences.s037-s19: The king of the world summoned three children.
sentences.s037-s20: Concerning that matter — Jandal's going and his own plan — he plainly laid out all his words before (the (king/assembly)).
sentences.s037-s9: By the king's command, these three children of mine will then come forth from my lineage.
sentences.s037-s10: Where shall I see your three kings who brighten your crown and throne?
sentences.s037-s11: If all three come near me, this dark city of mine will become bright.
sentences.s037-s12: My heart will be glad at their meeting; may I behold their awakened spirits.
sentences.s037-s13: I see them coming, their hearts full of justice; I take their hand in my hand as a warning.
sentences.s037-s14: Then I entrust my three bright world‑seers to them, according to my custom.
sentences.s037-s15: When the longing to see them comes, I send them lightly back toward the king.
sentences.s037-s16: When the poet of Jandal heard the reply, he kissed his throne as was fitting.
sentences.s037-s5: I will accept whatever words you say; I will judge by my daughter.
sentences.s037-s6: If the king wishes to see me, or (if) to see the roving plains and the throne of Yemen.
sentences.s037-s7: I will not see myself as more contemptible than his three children when they stand before (him).
sentences.s037-s8: So, if the king's desire is thus, it is not fitting to take a step except by his command.
sentences.s037-s21: Thus he said that this king of Yemen — the head of the assembly, a cypress casting shade.
sentences.s037-s22: When, like an unwrought gem, he had three daughters; he had no son — his daughter Afsar there was.
sentences.s037-s23: If the angel Soroush should find them fit as brides, he will give them as brides before each (suitor), except the dust-kisser.
sentences.s037-s24: For your sake I asked of the father; I have set forth fitting words.
sentences.s037-s25: Now you must turn to him and follow Farrokh's counsel in every matter, great or small.
sentences.s037-s26: Be a poet (or minstrel) and very shrewd, and set both ears to his speech (i.e., listen very attentively to what he says).
sentences.s037-s27: Answer his words well; when he asks, offer (or put forward) Farrokh's counsel.
sentences.s037-s28: For (or because) one reared by a king should be none other than pious.
sentences.s037-s29: Eloquent, clear‑hearted, and devout — foreseeing whatever affair may befall him.
sentences.s037-s30: His speech was arrayed with truth; his wisdom had made (others) stare and was lofty as a cloud of desire.
sentences.s037-s31: Hear from me whatever I say; if you carry out the work, it will be prosperous.
sentences.s037-s32: There is one deep‑seer, the king of Yemen, such that when he is not present at any gathering...
sentences.s037-s1: He summoned the king's envoy forward and uttered many words well.
sentences.s037-s2: Tell him that I, your sovereign, am humbler, and that in whatever he commands I am obedient.
sentences.s037-s3: Tell him that although you are lofty, your three sons are more honoured than you.
sentences.s037-s4: The king's own son was dear to him, especially because he was handsome.
